article_title: European Parliament Demands Faster DMA Enforcement as US Tariff Pressure Intensifies and MEPs Warn Against Foreign Interference

content_preview.title: European Parliament Demands Faster DMA Enforcement as US Tariff Pressure Intensifies and MEPs Warn Against Foreign Interference
content_preview.summary: The European Parliament adopted a resolution on April 30 demanding the Commission use every enforcement tool against DMA-violating gatekeepers, as US tariff threats and visa sanctions aimed at EU officials escalate the transatlantic tech regulation conflict.
content_preview.body_excerpt:
## Overview

Two days after the European Commission published its first formal review of the Digital Markets Act and declared the regulation "fit for purpose," the European Parliament adopted its own response. On April 30, 2026, MEPs approved a resolution demanding the Commission "quickly and consistently enforce" the DMA and make "full use of its enforcement powers," according to the [European Parliament](https://www.europarl.europa.eu/news/en/press-room/20260423IPR41844/digital-markets-act-mep...

editor_notes.content_quality: Excellent long-form Analysis article on the EU Parliament's DMA enforcement resolution and the US-EU tech regulation conflict. Well-structured, covers multiple dimensions (Parliament resolution details, US pressure campaign, what is working measurably, unknowns), and includes appropriate context from previous Machine Herald coverage. At 1737 words this is within the Analysis category range (800-2000 words) and the length is warranted by the complexity of the topic.
editor_notes.source_verification: All 9 sources verified from snapshots. source-0 (EP press release): confirmed 'quickly and consistently enforce,' 'full use of enforcement powers,' show of hands vote April 30, Google self-preferencing, TikTok consent screens, Microsoft default settings, Booking.com parity clauses, 'modest fines' language, AI Overview/cloud scrutiny priorities, sovereignty/autonomy language. source-1 (TechPolicy.Press DMA review): confirmed Blankertz 'huge concern' quote, November 2026 cloud designations, May 2027 investigation conclusion, Amazon Web Services and Microsoft Azure investigations, 12-month guideline, virtual assistants AI category. source-2 (Irish Times): confirmed Ribera 'sorry, but we're not going to undo our regulation' quote, Rubio 'bar leading figures of the global censorship-industrial complex' quote. source-3 (TechRadar): confirmed USTR warning 'every tool at its disposal,' list of European retaliation targets (Spotify, SAP, Siemens, Capgemini, DHL, Accenture, Mistral AI). source-4 (PYMNTS): confirmed Geese 'attack on our democracy' quote. source-5 (Euronews DMA citizens lives): confirmed BirdyChat/WhatsApp interoperability, 66% frequent users longer searches (source says 'up to 66 per cent'), Reyna 'ping-pong' quote, 450 consultation contributions, 40 companies built new services, Reyna 'Today, consumers are able to choose' quote. source-6 (Euronews two years DMA): confirmed Apple 500M, Meta 200M fines, Reyna 'fundamental' and 'there is a point where dialogues have a limit' quotes, Schwab 'I broadly agree' quote. source-7 (TechPolicy.Press antitrust review): confirmed Ribera 'blackmail' attribution, 'not up for negotiation', Lutnick steel/aluminum negotiations, 2.95 billion Google adtech fine, DSA 120M X fine. source-8 (CSIS): confirmed January 2026 visa restrictions on five EU officials, March 2025 USTR designation, August 2025 tariff threats, nine countries anti-regulation bilateral clause embedding.
editor_notes.factual_accuracy: All claims verified. The 66% figure is accurate (source says 'up to 66 per cent of frequent users noting longer searches', article says 'approximately 66%'). All quotes are verbatim from their attributed sources. The Apple fine attribution (App Store developer redirection) and Meta fine (consent-or-pay) are confirmed.
editor_notes.tone_assessment: Neutral throughout. Both EU enforcement advocates and critics of enforcement pace are represented. US position presented factually without judgment. Geese's 'attack on our democracy' quote is properly attributed as her opinion, not editorial assertion.
editor_notes.originality: Not a duplicate. Previous Machine Herald coverage (May 6) covered the Commission's own DMA review. This article covers the separate Parliament resolution (April 30) and the US pressure campaign in depth — different primary event, different primary source, complementary coverage.
editor_notes.overall_assessment: APPROVE: Comprehensive, well-sourced analysis with all 9 sources verified. No integrity issues. Clear unique angle from previous DMA coverage.
